question: What is the difference between Continuous Delivery and Continuous Deployment?

options.text: Continuous Delivery requires manual approval for production deploy, Continuous Deployment deploys automatically
Continuous Delivery is only testing, Continuous Deployment is only deploying
options.explanation: Both approaches include testing. The difference is whether manual approval is needed before production deploy

options.text: No difference — they are synonyms
options.explanation: These are different approaches: Delivery means code is always release-ready (manual deploy), Deployment means every commit automatically goes to production

options.text: Continuous Deployment only works with Docker, while Delivery works with any technology
options.explanation: Both approaches are technology-agnostic. The difference is in the degree of deploy automation

question: What image tagging strategy is recommended for CI/CD?

options.text: Semantic versioning (1.2.3) for production releases
Git commit hash for unique build identification
latest for all environments
options.explanation: latest is an unpredictable tag that can point to any version. CI/CD needs specific, reproducible tags

options.text: Branch and hash combination (main-abc123f) for intermediate builds

question: Why use layer caching when building Docker in CI/CD?

options.text: To speed up builds — unchanged layers (dependencies) are not rebuilt
To reduce image size
options.explanation: Caching doesn't affect final image size. It speeds up the build process by skipping unchanged layers

options.text: To improve build security
options.explanation: Caching is not related to security. Its purpose is to accelerate CI/CD pipelines

options.text: CI/CD systems require mandatory caching
options.explanation: Caching is optional but recommended for faster pipelines

question: What is a multi-platform build (docker buildx)?

options.text: Building one image for multiple architectures (amd64, arm64) from a single Dockerfile
Building an image for both Docker and Podman simultaneously
options.explanation: buildx is not related to Podman. It's a tool for cross-platform builds targeting different CPU architectures

options.text: Running one container on multiple servers simultaneously
options.explanation: That's an orchestrator's job (Kubernetes, Swarm). buildx builds images for different architectures

options.text: A synonym for multi-stage build
options.explanation: Multi-stage is multi-step build for optimization. Multi-platform is building for different CPU architectures (ARM, x86)

question: What problems does CI/CD automation solve for Docker applications?

options.text: Human error — forgotten tests, wrong branches, missed migrations
Build reproducibility — the same image for all environments
Image version control — knowing exactly what's deployed in production
Complete replacement of DevOps engineers — CI/CD manages infrastructure by itself
options.explanation: CI/CD automates routine processes but requires setup and maintenance by DevOps specialists
